title: Sluter, Well of Moses (quiz)
topic_path: Claus Sluter and Claus de Werve

assessment_items.question_content:

assessment_items.question_content:
**Sluter individualizes the faces and carves the drapery folds so that they fill the space around the figures, which marks a change from earlier Gothic sculpture.**

assessment_items.question_content:
**Which of the following is NOT true of Sluter's *Well of Moses*?**

![](${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/099e16562485e946413e33c660262e87.png)

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
**The figures were painted and some of this paint survives today.**

![](${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/04b6223a3a1afbf81fe07fb94c9e4ec1.png)

choices.content: Originally, this well stood in a cloister surrounded by the cells of Carthusian monks.
The patron, Philip the Bold, paid particular attention to the monastery, and he had instructed the monks to say prayers on his behalf.
The sculpture is hexagonal, showing a prophet on each side. Sluter's prophets emerge from their niches, instead of being confined by the niches.
The *Well of Moses* was left unpainted by Sluter, who wanted to emphasize the timelessness of the stone.

assessment_items.question_content:
**The prophets and angels have blank expressions on their faces, which inspired piety and serenity in the monks.**



![](${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/46343b83a6a49e063984a9d556fc67b4.png)

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: **The prophets and angels show emotion in their faces, which inspired the prayers of the monks.**
